Yeah the thing people don't realize about Wilder Worlds is their blueprint for their game is just an enhanced version of The Matrix demo game. author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: We’ve handled ourselves exceptionally. It’s a selfless gesture to turn the other cheek and it’s commendable. We have every right to return whatever they have thrown our way back at them. They crossed the line by baselessly implying that we were scamming people among other thing. As long as we don’t go beyond what they did to us in retaliation it’s totally fair. I’d like to add that we did take the high ground many times even to our detriment. Again we have acted impeccably. I would even go as far as using this as an example to teach my children. Take the high road when you can, and never go beyond what’s being done to you, but treat people the same way they treat you if you must.

author: ! ᚺEIM | VɪᴋɪɴɢᴇɴBᴇɴɴʏViking
content:
@Signal It’ll be interesting to see how it works out.  I take Chips real answer to be that he doesn’t know, and it’s an evolving topic.
Yes, combat is an evolving topic. We worked out new ways to do things on chain that we thought was off limits when I wrote the manual in December. 

Regardless, Fleet size isn't arbitrary because combat isn't the only reason one would care about the size of their fleet. What if one of your ships or crew has a perk that buffs all the other ships in your fleet? What if you are taking on an Career mission that limits it to one fleet? Or you want to level a crew member's commander skill and it requires a fleet size of some quantity to gain more experience? What if you want to leverage the warp distance of one of your large ships by putting smaller ships with it in a fleet? What if you don't want your smaller ships to get "one shot" so you increase the fleet size? What about stealth mechanics of a fleet? Food consumption if a ship in your fleet has hydroponics? 

The point is, fleet size is an intended mechanic that we would ensure has interesting trade-offs as part of player progression.

Regardless, Fleet size isn't arbitrary because combat isn't the only reason one would care about the size of their fleet. What if one of your ships or crew has a perk that buffs all the other ships in your fleet? What if you are taking on an Career mission that limits it to one fleet? Or you want to level a crew member's commander skill and it requires a fleet size of some quantity to gain more experience? What if you want to leverage the warp distance of one of your large ships by putting smaller ships with it in a fleet? What if you don't want your smaller ships to get "one shot" so you increase the fleet size? What about stealth mechanics of a fleet? Food consumption if a ship in your fleet has hydroponics?   The point is, fleet size is an intended mechanic that we would ensure has interesting trade-offs as part of player progression.

author: ZiggyMUD
content: Thank you Siggy!  I have been doing similar calcs, and this really helps see the big picture. With only 27MM Polis liquid, its apparent just how important the current emission payments are.  The 220,000 paid out daily is 8/10s of a percent of the liquid Polis available to trade.  Think about that, almost 1 percent of the available Polis is released every single day.  Plus DAO emissions, plus what the team was selling (suspended). Polis could only go down.   By year end though, with a playable game, things may be very different.

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
@Lorddo If you look at the ATLAS in circulation chart from the econ paper, you will see the red line is approximately where the vested ATLAS tokens stop being released. If we assume this is Sept 2023 then the amount of ATLAS that is supposed to be in circulation at this time is 20-22 Billion ATLAS. As you can see from the ATLAS locker the total ATLAS in circulation is only 13 Billion! Meaning we have only a little over half of the ATLAS in circulation than was expected.
Would having both SAGE Labs/REC operating while SCORE is too something that could help this number get back on track?
Has ATMTA assumed a different ATLAS in circulation curve?
Not trying to be disrespectful, I just feel these might be more important questions to ask in my opinion.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: Faster pace = bots scaling disproportionately. We can only hope that our local undercover botters are non extractive. We also get to choose when they have, and to what extent disproportionate advantages. One of the many ways to mitigate this risk is to increase the pace progressively over time. As the reward curve lowers (black line pictured above) we could increase the pace and prevent potentially extractive bots from scaling early on in the economy. Having legitimate players and guilds become the pillars of the economy is an important step towards decentralization. They also act as a buffer to the extractive automators that are sure to come, if not here already.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: once you give them the opportunity to scale disproportionately theres no stopping them. there are collectively more of them then there are devs, and they have more processing power, knowledge, and resources in every single facet. They know what the limits are and the tools devs use and how to stay under the radar. They will go exactly to the threshold that is available for them to extract, and then scale. They even have spare resources to test that boundary and loose assets in the process in order to optimize. it's a whole industry, and they are always on the lookout for honey pots in gamefi

content: Danny has been putting a ton of work getting a UGC skin market online. And it uses all of the resources you’re extracting in Labs.

author: 𝕘𝕣𝕚𝕤𝕥
content:
The value is it being an OG skin. So the idea is the owner is able to “pull” some of that value. Tagging it would reduce or destroy the value. The idea is based on how games offer recolors of OG skins. They still sell even though it’s just a recolor. You’d think players would reject them….they scoop them up. 

Who knows…I just think it’s cool seeing if it would work.

If OG skins won’t have value then this means our OG ships value will be reduced…since I believe the same thing will happen to OG ships.

author: AndrewB [GW]
content: on the topic of bots, fighting them is a pretty futile matter, especially at the scale that SA wants to get to. I think the best way deal with bots is to design systems that naturally resist botting behavior because using a bot would not be the most effective solution. A good way to do this would be to use a combination of timegating, diminishing returns on rewards, and soulbound progression. for example, you could use a rep system that has weekly caps and to hit the weekly cap you would need to spend X hrs per day with Y medium ships. smaller ships need more time, larger less time. Add 1 rep for each manufacturer and make the reps very important to progression. systems like this will naturally resist multi accounting because it makes sense to keep the ships doing those tasks in the same wallet. multi accounting is one of the pillars of effective botting. Now if someone wanted to keep up multiple accounts they would need more ships which means more $, which is the biggest deterrent for botting... increased capital investment. Next you need to make the rewards for non human play next to meaningless. A really nice way is to make the first couple of hours each day the most rewarding part of the play session. One thing the team should consider is figuring out how to add a daily reset to the chain programs. this will make it possible to implement diminishing returns on rewards/progression. could probably tie it to the Solana clock at some interval. speaking as a crazy gamer the max I can play is about 7am to 1am ish over the weekend with a quick lunch break if im really into a game of anno 1800 or dyson sphere program. after 2 days of that im pretty zonked... those would be fair to say non healthy amounts of hours. after 16 hours of effective/actual play (not afking) the game should be very minimally rewarding... if at all. if you got 100 rewards per 24 hour period and you were playing efficiently you should have 50 of those rewards in the fist 2 hours, 80 in the first 4 hours, 95 in the first 8 hours and 99 in the first 16 hours. this way if someone want to degen for a day they still have that meaningful progress but more then 4 hours of focused gaming per day is not the path to a healthy life... unless you don't have to work then you can do more. using this system we have worsened our multi account issue. we have added some multi account resistance above with the rep system but we need a bit more then that. An interesting way to do this... and i believe chypto mentioned this in the past... but crew need to rest to be effective. so if 8 hours per day is used to rest and reset your crew you have made a pretty large impact to multi accounting as at least 12-16 hours a day a ship can be in one wallet be operated by a human and keeping pace with a robot efficiently. you could also include different mechanics that would reward keeping ships in single wallets that would not punish too hard for wallet swapping... like if you rested your ship for the full 8 hours, when you next undock you ship it gains a buff to extraction rate/fire rate/'other stuff' rate and that buff is deleted when it leaves the wallet. Another way to do this would be to have a waiting timer when you first add a ship to your wallet. say you buy a ship/move one into your wallet, it could need 1 hour to "configure it's systems" to be ready to undock from the CSS. This would slow down wallet hopping for the best rewards if the rewards system mentioned above was used. using some of these design elements do not directly fight botting but they make botting less necessary/efficient. bots will never go away. but if you make the barriers to entry large enough and the potential rewards small enough there will not be that many and the game will still be really awesome... even though technically we can't play and earn 24/7 at an even rate of return. 24/7 unlimited rewards leads to FOMO that ends in burnout, not healthy for the game. if you made it to the end thanks for reading!!

author: 𝕘𝕣𝕚𝕤𝕥
content: In a game with hundreds of thousands of concurrent players OG skins will be rare. That’s what makes them OG. You can only rent one skin to one player. The supply doesn’t change. Would still be OG (these are all opinions. This sort of market is new so it’s all theory)

author: [EMP] jith blade
content:
but yeah the best way to deal with bots is make the entire thing bot resistant, I do think that the diminishing returns under the current idea for sec zone is going to work as they are reduced global returns on the individual level so after a point adding in more bots will not do anything (as the actual max amount produced by a planet is fixed the more people drawing from that get proportionately lower returns for the same level of development meaning the only solution is to ramp up development vs adding in more claims after a certain threshold is reached) 
also going into mrz opens you up to pvp and I am going to be honest nobody is going to care about a bounty generated for killing bots if anything we would likely have services that help wash those bounties that the game auto generates

author: [EMP] jith blade
content: also factions could do things like vote in restrictions on sales and pricing for suspected bots

This would slow down wallet hopping for the best rewards if the rewards system mentioned above was used. using some of these design elements do not directly fight botting but they make botting less necessary/efficient. bots will never go away. but if you make the barriers to entry large enough and the potential rewards small enough there will not be that many and the game will still be really awesome... even though technically we can't play and earn 24/7 at an even rate of return. 24/7 unlimited rewards leads to FOMO that ends in burnout, not healthy for the game. if you made it to the end thanks for reading!!

I think the best way deal with bots is to design systems that naturally resist botting behavior because using a bot would not be the most effective solution. A good way to do this would be to use a combination of timegating, diminishing returns on rewards, and soulbound progression. for example, you could use a rep system that has weekly caps and to hit the weekly cap you would need to spend X hrs per day with Y medium ships. smaller ships need more time, larger less time. Add 1 rep for each manufacturer and make the reps very important to progression. systems like this will naturally resist multi accounting because it makes sense to keep the ships doing those tasks in the same wallet. multi accounting is one of the pillars of effective botting. Now if someone wanted to keep up multiple accounts they would need more ships which means more $, which is the biggest deterrent for botting... increased capital investment. Next you need to make the rewards for non human play next to meaningless. A really nice way is to make the first couple of hours each day the most rewarding part of the play session. One thing the team should consider is figuring out how to add a daily reset to the chain programs. this will make it possible to implement diminishing returns on rewards/progression. could probably tie it to the Solana clock at some interval. speaking as a crazy gamer the max I can play is about 7am to 1am ish over the weekend with a quick lunch break if im really into a game of anno 1800 or dyson sphere program. after 2 days of that im pretty zonked... those would be fair to say non healthy amounts of hours. after 16 hours of effective/actual play (not afking) the game should be very minimally rewarding... if at all. if you got 100 rewards per 24 hour period and you were playing efficiently you should have 50 of those rewards in the fist 2 hours, 80 in the first 4 hours, 95 in the first 8 hours and 99 in the first 16 hours. this way if someone want to degen for a day they still have that meaningful progress but more then 4 hours of focused gaming per day is not the path to a healthy life... unless you don't have to work then you can do more. using this system we have worsened our multi account issue. we have added some multi account resistance above with the rep system but we need a bit more then that. author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: something that happened at Axie a lot was giving your assets to other accounts and play 20 accounts with the same assets,  to prevent this they implemented a 1 day cooldown before assets were useable in-game. I would hate a cooldown like that here because when you buy that awesome next step of a space ship you want to fly it straight away.   But recalibrating crew (job training)  or repainting your asset so it wears your own tag, could be a way to implement something like that, maybe give a borrow/dummy ship that isnt able to earn yet so you can fly but dont earn yet, if you break it, you buy it (they still hold yours so they would just cancel the trade on it and you would lose the asset). but its a double edge sword,  trying to combat the bots and not taking the fun out of it for the gamers  I think your right about battling bots is a requirement if thats the only way to earn, but even with score your still fighting that same fight, as they will be here for sure, they controlled the blackmarket in other games, sure of as hell they will not give up their market so easily. They are located at different regions so they can work for WAY less then any of us, specially because they will be running bots on all kind of crypto games, not just one where We are spending all our time.

author: Lorddo [ALRT]
content: on the topic of bots, fighting them is a pretty futile matter, especially at the scale that SA wants to get to. I think the best way deal with bots is to design systems that naturally resist botting behavior because using a bot would not be the most effective solution. A good way to do this would be to use a combination of timegating, diminishing returns on rewards, and soulbound progression. for example, you could use a rep system that has weekly caps and to hit the weekly cap you would need to spend X hrs per day with Y medium ships. smaller ships need more time, larger less time. Add 1 rep for each manufacturer and make the reps very important to progression. systems like this will naturally resist multi accounting because it makes sense to keep the ships doing those tasks in the same wallet. multi accounting is one of the pillars of effective botting. Now if someone wanted to keep up multiple accounts they would need more ships which means more $, which is the biggest deterrent for botting... increased capital investment. Next you need to make the rewards for non human play next to meaningless. A really nice way is to make the first couple of hours each day the most rewarding part of the play session. One thing the team should consider is figuring out how to add a daily reset to the chain programs. this will make it possible to implement diminishing returns on rewards/progression. could probably tie it to the Solana clock at some interval. speaking as a crazy gamer the max I can play is about 7am to 1am ish over the weekend with a quick lunch break if im really into a game of anno 1800 or dyson sphere program. after 2 days of that im pretty zonked... those would be fair to say non healthy amounts of hours. after 16 hours of effective/actual play (not afking) the game should be very minimally rewarding... if at all. if you got 100 rewards per 24 hour period and you were playing efficiently you should have 50 of those rewards in the fist 2 hours, 80 in the first 4 hours, 95 in the first 8 hours and 99 in the first 16 hours. this way if someone want to degen for a day they still have that meaningful progress but more then 4 hours of focused gaming per day is not the path to a healthy life... unless you don't have to work then you can do more. using this system we have worsened our multi account issue. we have added some multi account resistance above with the rep system but we need a bit more then that. An interesting way to do this... and i believe chypto mentioned this in the past... but crew need to rest to be effective. so if 8 hours per day is used to rest and reset your crew you have made a pretty large impact to multi accounting as at least 12-16 hours a day a ship can be in one wallet be operated by a human and keeping pace with a robot efficiently. you could also include different mechanics that would reward keeping ships in single wallets that would not punish too hard for wallet swapping... like if you rested your ship for the full 8 hours, when you next undock you ship it gains a buff to extraction rate/fire rate/'other stuff' rate and that buff is deleted when it leaves the wallet. Another way to do this would be to have a waiting timer when you first add a ship to your wallet. say you buy a ship/move one into your wallet, it could need 1 hour to "configure it's systems" to be ready to undock from the CSS. This would slow down wallet hopping for the best rewards if the rewards system mentioned above was used. using some of these design elements do not directly fight botting but they make botting less necessary/efficient. bots will never go away. but if you make the barriers to entry large enough and the potential rewards small enough there will not be that many and the game will still be really awesome... even though technically we can't play and earn 24/7 at an even rate of return. 24/7 unlimited rewards leads to FOMO that ends in burnout, not healthy for the game. if you made it to the end thanks for reading!!

author: Xcode [STAR]
content:
import requests

galaxy = "https://galaxy.staratlas.com/items"
galaxy_response = requests.get(galaxy)
# produce a json formatted copy of galaxy data to work with
galaxy_json_dict = galaxy_response.json()


# k represents each entry in the galaxy database
for k in galaxy_json_dict:
    # search for all the posters in galaxy
    if k['attributes']['class'] == 'poster':
        # and print a CSV formatted output of the posters 'name' and 'audio'
        print(k['name'] + ", " + k['media']['audio'])
    else:
        pass

For you python geeks out there.

author: 𝕘𝕣𝕚𝕤𝕥
content:
Jensen drops the announcement of Nvidia AI Workbench at 107:00

Been thinking about how we get it all. Everything. 

Trying to pretend I wasn’t emotionally connected..all the dreams of what could be…

Watch the keynote. It’s like all the theory crafting that has been done here over the years is seemingly within reach.

I was bummed hearing about the layoffs. I think anyone who wants to come back will have their chance.

Go play Journey if you have never played it. There’s no game like it to me….that form of PVP was crazy to me the first play through. Less then 20 people created Journey.

And they didn’t have anything close to the dev tools Team will be utilizing over the next two years.

I’m back to fanboi Level 9000

Edit: seriously if you haven’t played Journey do it now.

content: I think it’ll all be a lot more complex than that.  Star Bases could burn and consume their own resources at a rate relative to what’s happening there and have to resupply themselves to provide the capacity for you to craft there at all. Also again, I don’t think you’ll pay any of this if you build your own base and just craft there.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: The problem here is that the tax on crafting is said to be based on market value, and is applied at every tree level of craft recipe even if assets don't change hands. So if you craft and have to raise prices to keep making a profit, the tax goes up. And so then you have to increase prices again. If it's a fee that isn't based on market value, you don't have this immediate feedback effect. The cost to producing an item should not be immediately sensitive to how much people are paying for it.  ALSO people crafting for themselves should not be subject to the market value of a particular item when crafting items on their own. That concept is mind boggling to me. It's fine to have a relatively stable facility usage fee (until that is later decided by P2P or regional DAO SB stuff), but a tax on creating things for your own usage. eh. Imagine gathering a bunch of materials for a month to craft a big ship, and then a day before you get everything ready to craft it, the market value on that ship jumps. Now you can't afford to craft it.  Am I missing why market value based taxes on crafting are a good thing?

reply.preview: The problem here is that the tax on crafting is said to be based on market value, and is applied at every tree level of craft recipe even if assets don't change hands. Imagine gathering a bunch of materials for a month to craft a big ship, and then a day before you get everything ready to craft it, the market value on that ship jumps. Now you can't afford to craft it. Am I missing why market value based taxes on crafting are a good thing?

author: Signal
content: @SW4GN3R flip side dashboard implies on average right now we are providing 88% of daily R4 demand via passive staking.  The current stockpile of R4 is enough for at least 105 days of current demand, and that’s not counting SAGE Labs jumping in and adding to these numbers.  Higher prices drive opportunity costs for claim owners, I personally just don’t see it.  We were meeting like 40% of daily demand back when I drafted PIP-X and I was told the markets didn’t need the DAO’s help.  On its own, supply has reached 88% of demand since then.  If my PIP was insane to propose back then (many said it was), intervention now would seem truly ludicrous.

author: Signal
content: But by all means, folks can let me know if they want PIP-X back

author: Signal
content: I’d wager they don’t

author: Signal
content: **And for the record at this point, I don’t want it back either.  The people were right, free markets solved the problem.  I underestimated how much cash and willingness was out there to buy these things up.  Folks proved me wrong with their paychecks

author: [AEP] Boen
content: Problem is the team wants / needs people to buy the miners and claim stakes to generate some revenue, and the R4 production relative to demand is likely optimized for the demand lever to be towards miners / claim stakes, if not enough of them have been sold into the market and thus insufficient r4 production vs deman occurs and R4 prices go up. I think it is good we are seeing a bit of R4 deficit and price increase as it focuses the economic need on more production to be introduced, which is beneficial to revenue generation for the team. And we definitely still have a lot of potential R4 supply from unsold miners / CS

author: [AEP] Boen
content:
The problem is the amount stockpiled versus the daily production deficit (10B to 15B per R4 on average vs a deficit on ammo and tools of 29M and 35M respectively). The supply is there, it is just been locked up. If that were to be released if would crater prices. Kind of like the US federal reserve pumping money into the economy and basically pushing on a wet noodle over the last 10 years. Lots of money supply but no velocity of money in the economy. And inflation is not just based on supply but the velocity of that supply through the economy. 
Hopefully SAGE will provide some trigger where people realize they don't need to stockpile as much R4 as they are doing so and instead devote some of that locked up capital into R9 or crafting, and increased supply of R4 also comes onto the market

author: [AEP] Boen
content: Manipulation is a tough word to use but back when EV was sunsetting there was a lot of wallets buying and hoarding. Some didn’t have to many ships. Some smaller wallets with no assets feeding larger wallets too. Also a few swing traders tried to control the spread and there was almost no selling on the market, mostly just buying and they would wipe out the sell side after accumulating some millions of r4 to move the spread in their favor and would put large orders on buy side and always tried to stay on top even if the spread was below their market fee levels. Things like that but ultimately i noticed that there was almost no selling compared to buying. I was able to catch 50-60 million of each r4 on the second to last loot drop (before redistribution) and also about 20-30 million more over two weeks about so I could try to push the price down but demand was just crazy overall. Hard to pinpoint the overall situation but there was just a lot more demand than supply going into the marketplace it felt like. Along with hoarding from a handful of wallets as well. Doesn’t paint the whole picture I’m sure but that’s what I noticed when I tried to help the transition from EV to Labs

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I think my confusion is on SA Listed Prices that include a discount vs VWAP Prices.   Let's take the TIP for example:  VWAP:  860 SA Listed Price: 430  Using SA Listed Price Referral gets 10% off:  Sold at 430 - 43 = 387 Referrer gets 25% of the 387 = 96.75  Total Discount:  43 + 96.75 = 139.75  For someone self-referring, total price & discount is 430 - 139.75 = 290.25 or 32.5% discount off  SA Listed price.  Using VWAP Price Referral gets 10% off:  Sold at 860 -86 = 774 Referrer gets 25% of the 387 = 193.5  Total Discount:  86 + 193.5 = 279.5  For someone self-referring, total price & discount is (860 - 279.5) = 580.5 or 32.5% discount off VWAP.  Conclusion: *If SA Listed prices are used, self-referring traders could re-list the asset well below SA Listed price & profit handsomely - promoting the tactic.  (Note: The same logic applies to other mining ships, titans, and any similarly discounted assets.   @𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul  @SW4GN3R

content: it should be temporary try these steps. if it doesnt help ⁠No Access  Sometimes, connectivity problems can be resolved by resetting your router or VPN to obtain a new IP address. It's a simple but effective step that often fixes connection-related issues. Clear Cache and Cookies: If you're encountering slow loading times or strange behavior on StarComm, it's a good idea to clear your browser's cache and cookies. This can help refresh the page and remove any stored data that might be causing conflicts. Try Another Browser or Device: Occasionally, certain browsers or devices may not be fully compatible with StarComm. Switching to a different browser or using an alternate device (e.g., from a computer to a phone) can help you identify if the problem is specific to your setup.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: The reason we can't answer exactly how many hangars and how many ships of X type fit in said hangar questions is because we need to be much further along in ship metrics and interiors across our vast inventory. Take the F4 for example. We started interior design work on it months ago. In parallel, Ship Config V2 was being defined. This meant we started on some interiors before we knew exactly what the ships needed in the interior to be balanced against other ships in the same tier. Once we finished up V2, we reworked some interior elements on a few ships. As we worked on the interiors, most ships got bigger (F4 certainly did) to accommodate everything they needed including Usturs walking through doors. With several ships changing sizes, hangar metrics (sizes of different pads) have had to change. It was only until the last few months where nailed down hangar metrics a bit more to apply to the C9 layout. Our foundations are solidifying. This means it will be easier to progress on the other ships when we get to them. Currently, we are focusing on smaller ships to support our upcoming racing gamemodes.

author: Odicerous
content:
To get a little perspective  on just how big the C11 is, take a look at an Oasis class cruise ship.  They're the biggest in the world.The Symphony of the Seas is 361 meters long, 66 meters abeam (widest), 72 meters tall. 
I'll guestimate the exterior structure occupies 60% of that measurement envelope, so it has roughly 1,000,000 cubic meters of interior volume.  Its massive.
It holds a crew of 2,200 people, and another 6,000 guests.  It has every amenity imaginable, plus engines and maintenance areas, and back off house facilities.  Its a floating city.

author: Odicerous
content:
As currently spec'd, the C11 is 2.2 times as long.  4.4 times as wide (at widest).  2.2 times as tall.  If the shape of the C11 is narrower, and only uses say, 45% of the measurement envelope, it would still be 17 million cubic meters.That is 16 times more volume than the worlds largest cruise ship.
I've been on that ship.  The concept of something even 10 times more voluminous is absolutely mind blowing.
I think the devs have some room to play with.  The scale of the C11 should be incredible.

⁠archived-foundation-room⁠  @PG I BULK  In EV the playing field was equalized by activity since ship/fleet size didn't matter - it was intensely focused on labor.  In SAGE Labs, size of ships begins to matter.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: ⁠archived-foundation-room⁠  @MrTixl~ Finding, Building and Testing the programs/tools (constructing primitives) that can reliably be implemented in both SAGE & UE5 and Solana blockchain were the main time eaters.  The SA Team began work in untested waters. And trailblazing is time consuming. SAGE Labs begins the process of combining  game theory (which was already outlined/refined in 2021-22) with actual blockchain technology intertwined.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: ⁠archived-foundation-room⁠  @Deleted User  Putting things in perspective, I  remember Summer 2021 (June-Aug) Townhalls where the O-Game idea was already being discussed as an initial way to implement a very simple game (much of the game economics were in theory crafting).  The focus in 2021 became Faction Fleet (released on Dec 2021) because people demanded a way to stake the ships sold during the GAO rounds.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content:
MORE --> Follow on to SCORE, O-game like idle gameplay, quickly abandoned in late 2021 at the concept and early prototype phase.

CREAM --> Crafting, resource extraction and mining. Was going to be Claim stake centered, and was a fleshed out design document. But we decided to focus on Ships instead in early 2022. Some of that claim stake design work was reused for the appendix in the SAGE manual in Dec 2022.

SCREAM --> Ship Combat, Resource extraction and mining was born Q1 2022. Around this time we were looking into different game engines and landed on Playcanvas since we wanted to leverage WebGL to keep it accessible.

SAGE --> Star Atlas Golden Era official rebranding of SCREAM, anounced at Breakpoint 2022. Entire v0 GDD complete and released in the SAGE Manuals Dec 2022.

Sage EV --> On-chain movement

Sage Labs --> On-chain movement, crafting, mining, scanning. Static simpler UI in order to release soon.

Sage Starbased --> Aka REC is Sage Labs with a 3d interface built in Playcanvas

author: 《VΛ》Keplernet - NebularTV
content:
looking at a drawing is way easier, but it's about a sphere centered on the zero coordinates and then:
r = radius of sphere passing through the actual point being considered. 
theta would be the angle, in an "horizontal" 2D plane, between a zero degree direction and the projection of a line going from the center of coordinates to the point (the radius line)
phi: we coud say that would be the "vertical angle" , so how far that radius line is moved up from that "horizontal" 2D plane

author: defiantwyvern
content: I think the idea of capturing time in an NFT and making it tradeable could be quite interesting. Time definitely has an implicit impact in modern world economics. For example the 'time' it took the United States to advance its level of technology from X to Y may be 100 years for example. But developing nations can cut that time in half to reach that same technological competency through economics, trade agreements, illicit actions. And often leapfrog tech that the front runner had to implement (such as go from very little communication infrastructure to a mass cellular network without having to lay down telephone lines for 50 years like the US did.

author: Kr1gs
content: I would say that production of resources through claimstakes and mining ships that are staked, is evenly distributed, as in, if you buy any of the claimstakes or mining ships, then you are receive commodities from the mining that the piece of capital that you have purchased, in proportion to what mining assets(s) you have purchased.  To my information, there is no restriction on who can purchase these assets, and the most inexpensive mining asset as of today, is approximately 32 USDC for a '"Claimstake 1".  Also, it is proper for a functional economy, i.e. one that mostly "works" to not give away something for nothing.  If people want the reward, they can pay the price for the reward.

author: Kr1gs
content: I think I understand your sentiment.  I also think that one of the several characteristics and goals of a functioning economy, is to have a broad middle class, and smaller lower and middle classes.  The thing is, at this stage, there is not even really a game yet: all of this is still being built.  What builds the overwhelming majority of things of any scale, is the investment, of and in capital, by investors.  By definition, the people with the extra funds to put into or commit .... to invest in something like this Star Atlas, are people with some extra money.  Hopefully once something is built, that gamers and others, other new investors find fun and rewarding, then ... hopefully this will skew more in the direction of having a larger "middle class".  These are my own thoughts, at least.  I think you are correct at the moment, and although I would say it is necessary and improbable to happen in another way, I hope "we" in Star Atlas attract from across the general public.  "Bigly"

author: ROME | Saint-JustPirate Y'aaar
content: To further elaborate, I was commenting on the number of productions held per individual compared to the number of wallets/users/consumers.   While yes, there is nothing restricting individuals from purchasing production assets (other than spare capital), I would assess that if broken down, a good number and percentage of total production is still held by a relatively small number of individuals comparatively.   That will likely change with Labs coming online, however I am still curiously interested in the percentage of production balance across number of users.   As such I would still assess the Star Atlas ecosystem as a Plutocracy.  Nothing in my statement implied giving away production assets for free.

author: [AEP] Boen
content:
I think yes and no on the zero sum game concept. if this stays purely as a 'gaming' environment, then yes I think it is a zero sum game. But wealth generation in an economy based on today's current measure of GDP is 1) productivity gains and 2) population growth. We hope the SA economy will see population growth and more dollars in the pot, a higher velocity of money in the system and higher demand should theoretically help the early adopters. But that will only represent a 'growing' economy so long as population growth continues. 
This is why irl you see european countries and places like Japan and Russia, facing demographic stagnation or population decline and a potential impact on their GDP potential

author: [AEP] Boen
content:
But this is where  productivity gains as part of the economic model really creates economic growth - ie simply doing more with less. And that 'less' can be fewer resource inputs to create the desired product or less time required on input thus allowing for 'time' to be utilized in other consumptive manners. 
Really the question is will the SA economy have structures in place that take advantage not just of population growth (the 'easy' part) but productivity mechanisms that lead to real wealth creation in a manner that ties into real world needs. I believe this is where the meta play comes into its greatest potential - the ability to utilize virtual environments to create experiences and transactional environments for people that don't have the time, physical or financial means to conduct those experiences on a real world basis 100% of their desired time

author: Eoganacht | BULK [BULK]
content:
@PG I BULK Rainbow lore is understood.

If you read the discussion yesterday, you will see people saying the Om should be better in every category except cargo capacity.

This doesn't make the Om worse. The Om can be a better freighter without having higher capacity.

Like many have pointed out, there is choice in having a fast/lower-cargo option and a slow/ higher-cargo option. Yesterday's stats provided one choice - Om.

This was not about making byos greater than Om. It was about saving the packlite from being never used, or significantly sidelined for its vwap.

From my perspective, the team demonstrated their best in class responsiveness to concerns. This is something blizzard wouldn't do imo, and it's once of the main reasons I believe in this team.

content: @JayWalk I think there will be a whole range of levels you can play at with SAGE. We could compare it to playing some of the 4X games out there like civilization, stellaris and others. author: [AEP] WindSplitter1 [AEP]
content:
I understand that part. And yes, fandom groups are there to create those tools. Some which have already done so.

But in order to make them, they require [updated] information to work on.

Like certain members were going on an overdrive over cargo space on a Packlite. Lack of hard data leads to a lot of speculation which isn't based on accurate facts.

Or, for the purposes of this conversation, "cargo space" is defined by "XXS" through "Titan". We don't know whether a M-Sized cargo bay will only accommodate a couple of Starfield sandwiches or 100t of gold ore.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Dom….I mean it has an 88 on metacritic with some depressing reviews (IGNs). I thought all the reviewers would be shilling. Kissing up to Bethesda.  Honestly what bugged me was someone saying the combat was bad. No recoil, no sway…just hit scan. But the only way for me to know is to play it. Just think the old way games are made is dead.  We get to see every twist and turn and feel part of the process.   The old way is rumor and wait. And as the years pass your mind builds up a game no dev could live up to.

author: 𝕘𝕣𝕚𝕤𝕥
content: It's definitely a lot easier said than done.  Keeping development as open as we do is a big risk, to be completely honest. Someone can see something that isn't finished that we're showing and say "That looks like shit, why would anyone play that?" and use it as fuel to shit on us. People with an audience, big and small, have already done this to us.   You also have to keep in mind that the people who work on stuff like this have a reputation they need to uphold. So risking that, showing something they did that is unfinished, can not only have early unwanted criticism but could also piss off the person who worked on it.  This is just to say I don't blame other game companies who don't do it and there's a reason it's standard.   I will say though, I wouldn't change our approach for the world, I'm just passing along some insight.

reply.preview: It's definitely a lot easier said than done. Keeping development as open as we do is a big risk, to be completely honest. Someone can see something that isn't finished that we're showing and say "That looks like shit, why would anyone play that?" and use it as fuel to shit on us. People with an audience, big and small, have already done this to us. You also have to keep in mind that the people who work on stuff like this have a reputation they need to uphold. So risking that, showing something they did that is unfinished, can not only have early unwanted criticism but could also piss off the person who worked on it. This is just to say I don't blame other game companies who don't do it and there's a reason it's standard. I will say though, I wouldn't change our approach for the world, I'm just passing along some insight.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
As we are off topic here, I will contribute my 2 cents as well 

Just read a review of Starfield on Polygon and I find their prime issue super interesting.

They say the game has so many procedural quests and encounters that these water down the the more meaningful and fulfilling main quests.

Or in other words: having more of the same quests is not only making the game not better, it is actually taking away from the game.
